articles.title: Chikkaballapur silk farmers hit by heat and price crash - The Hans India
articles.full_text:
Chikkaballapur silk farmers hit by heat and price crash
Chikkaballapur: The district is witnessing severe distress in agriculture as the intense summer heat continues to affect both people and livestock. Among the worst hit is the silk farming sector, one of Karnataka’s key commercial agricultural activities, which is now struggling with declining quality and a sudden fall in prices.
The ongoing heatwave has created extremely difficult conditions for silk rearing farmers. Sericulture, being a highly sensitive activity, requires controlled temperature conditions for silkworms to develop properly. However, rising temperatures have disrupted the growth cycle, resulting in lower productivity and poor-quality cocoons.
Farmers report that the expected yield has significantly dropped this season. The extreme heat has also led to increased disease outbreaks among silkworms, further affecting production.
Maintaining ideal conditions for rearing, typically between 28°C and 30°C, has become a major challenge under current weather conditions.
Along with production issues, the market price of silk cocoons has witnessed a steep decline. Around two weeks ago, silk cocoons were selling for nearly ₹800 per kilogram. However, the price has now fallen sharply to a maximum of ₹500 per kilogram. Farmers say this drop of nearly ₹300–₹400 per kilogram has severely impacted their income.
Interestingly, the supply in the market has not increased significantly, but traders are offering lower prices citing poor quality. The reduced weight and weakened texture of cocoons due to heat stress have contributed to this decline in value.
To protect their crops, farmers are adopting various adaptive measures. Many have constructed temporary shade structures around rearing units, installed shade nets, and used wet gunny cloths to cool the environment. Some have also introduced drip irrigation and regular water spraying to maintain humidity levels. Despite these efforts, maintaining optimal rearing conditions remains extremely difficult.
Rising input costs combined with falling prices have placed farmers under severe financial pressure. Many are now uncertain about continuing silk farming in the coming seasons unless market conditions improve.
